sections.heading: Background: The Mennonite Tradition
sections.html:
Background: The Mennonite Tradition

The Dordrecht Confession of 1632 is the most widely used and authoritative confession of the Mennonite and Amish traditions. Written in the Dutch city of Dordrecht on April 21, 1632, it represents the mature expression of the Anabaptist theological tradition that had developed across the preceding century since the foundational Schleitheim Confession of 1527 — yet it emerged not from a moment of crisis and persecution, as Schleitheim had, but from an attempt at reconciliation within a divided community.

To understand Dordrecht, one must understand what happened to the Anabaptist movement between 1527 and 1632. The early Swiss and South German Anabaptist movement had been shaped by persecution — the drowning of Felix Manz (1527), the burning of Michael Sattler (1527), and the systematic execution of thousands of Anabaptist believers across the Holy Roman Empire. But in the Netherlands, the movement took a different shape. Dutch Anabaptism included both peaceful Anabaptists in the Schleitheim tradition and a violent millenarian strain associated with the Münster Rebellion (1534–35), in which radical Anabaptists led by Jan of Leiden seized the city of Münster, instituted polygamy and a theocratic terror, and were eventually besieged and massacred. The Münster disaster permanently discredited violent Anabaptism and left the peaceful majority of Dutch Anabaptists in urgent need of a leader who could both repair the movement's reputation and give it positive theological definition.

That leader was Menno Simons (1496–1561), a former Catholic priest from Friesland who converted to the Anabaptist cause in 1537, two years after Münster. Menno was not a systematic theologian in the academic tradition, but he was a gifted pastor, writer, and organizer. Over the next quarter-century, traveling constantly under conditions of danger and persecution, he gathered and strengthened the scattered peaceful Anabaptist communities of the Netherlands and northern Germany. His followers became known as Mennisten — Mennonites — a name that within decades displaced the pejorative "Anabaptist" as the primary designator of the tradition.

Menno's theological legacy was genuinely peaceful, biblicist, and christocentric. He emphasized nonresistance, the gathered church of baptized believers, simple piety, and strict church discipline — including the practice of avoidance or shunning of excommunicated members, which would become the most contested aspect of the Mennonite tradition. But Menno himself had not produced a comprehensive systematic confession; the tradition he shaped lacked a single authoritative doctrinal statement.

By the early seventeenth century the Dutch Mennonite communities had divided into two main groups: the Flemish Mennonites (originally from Flanders, who tended toward stricter discipline) and the Frisian Mennonites (from Friesland, the area of Menno's own origin, somewhat more lenient). These groups had been in division since the 1560s over various disciplinary matters. The Dordrecht Confession was written by Adriaen Cornelisz, a Flemish Mennonite elder, with the explicit purpose of providing a shared confession that could reunite the divided communities. On April 21, 1632, at a meeting in Dordrecht, fifty-one Flemish and Frisian ministers signed the document, formally agreeing to it as the expression of their shared faith.

The Confession consists of eighteen articles organized in a roughly catechetical order, moving from God and creation through sin, redemption, the church, ordinances, discipline, the Christian life, and last things. It is more comprehensive than Schleitheim — a full-scale confession rather than a set of boundary markers — but its distinctive emphases (baptism, the Lord's Supper, footwashing, marriage, the ban, the oath, nonresistance) are exactly those that distinguish the Anabaptist-Mennonite tradition from the magisterial Reformation traditions.

sections.heading: Distinctive Doctrines: The Eighteen Articles
sections.html:
Distinctive Doctrines: The Eighteen Articles

The Dordrecht Confession's eighteen articles move from standard Reformed-adjacent affirmations about God, creation, and redemption to the distinctly Anabaptist practices that defined the tradition's identity in the seventeenth century and beyond.

Articles 1–3 — God, the Fall, and the Restoration. The opening articles affirm one God, eternal and triune; the creation of humanity in the divine image; the fall of Adam and Eve through disobedience; and the consequent condition of spiritual death and separation from God that marks all their descendants. These articles are broadly catholic and broadly in line with the Reformed confessional tradition, though without the technical philosophical precision of the Westminster Confession. The Confession moves quickly from the problem of human sin to its solution: God in his mercy did not abandon humanity but promised the Seed who would bruise the serpent's head (Gen. 3:15), and in the fullness of time sent his Son to accomplish redemption.

Articles 4–6 — Christ and Redemption. Christ is affirmed as the Son of God, born of the Virgin Mary, fully human and fully divine. His death is a genuine atonement — he bore the sins of the world and made peace between God and humanity. Through his resurrection he opened the way of life. The Confession does not use the technical vocabulary of Reformed soteriology (double predestination, limited atonement) — it presents the atonement in the broader evangelical terms of a ransom and a sacrifice, available to all who receive it in faith.

Article 7 — Holy Baptism. Baptism belongs to those who have repented of their sins and who believe in Christ for the remission of those sins. It is explicitly not to be administered to infants, who cannot repent or believe: "all penitent believers, who, through faith, repentance, and the new birth, have become united with God, and whose names are recorded in heaven, must, upon such scriptural confession of their faith and renewal of life, be baptized with water." Baptism is an outward sign of an inward reality — the burial and resurrection of the new life in Christ. The pattern is the apostolic one: "Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins" (Acts 2:38). (Acts 2:38; Rom. 6:4)

Article 8 — The Lord's Supper. The Lord's Supper is a memorial and proclamation of Christ's death, celebrated among baptized believers only. The Confession follows the broadly Zwinglian-memorialist interpretation — the Supper is a commemoration and a sign of fellowship among the body. Unlike the Lutheran tradition, there is no affirmation of Real Presence in the elements; unlike the Roman tradition, there is no sacrifice. The Supper is also an act of self-examination: each communicant reflects on whether they are rightly related to God and to their brothers and sisters.

Article 10 — Footwashing. One of the most distinctive features of the Dordrecht Confession — and one that sets it apart from virtually every other Protestant confession — is its treatment of footwashing as an ordinance of the church, required of all believers. The Confession states: "we consider it to be a practice binding upon all believers, since Christ did not merely wash the feet of his disciples but commanded them to wash one another's feet." The basis is the explicit command of Jesus: "If I then, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet. For I have given you an example, that you also should do just as I have done to you" (John 13:14–15). (John 13:14–15)

Article 11 — Marriage. Marriage is to be contracted only "in the Lord" — that is, with a fellow believer. A member may not marry an unbeliever, a person of another confession, or a person under the ban. This restriction, grounded in the Pauline injunction against being unequally yoked, was not merely a pious recommendation but an enforceable community norm: a member who married outside the community could be subject to church discipline. (2 Cor. 6:14)

Article 14 — The Ban and Avoidance. This article is at once the most contested and the most historically consequential in the entire Confession. The ban (excommunication) is grounded in Matthew 18 and Paul's instruction to "not associate with anyone who bears the name of brother if he is guilty of sexual immorality or greed, or is an idolater, reviler, drunkard, or swindler — not even to eat with such a one" (1 Cor. 5:11). The Confession goes beyond merely excluding an excommunicated member from the Lord's Supper; it specifies avoidance: members must shun — avoid social contact with — those who have been banned. This includes avoidance "at the table and in marriage bed" — even a spouse who has been excommunicated is to be avoided in these intimate contexts until they repent and are restored.

This provision for strict avoidance was not universally practiced in the Anabaptist tradition before Dordrecht, and its inclusion in the Confession — and above all its strictest application to the marital relationship — became the flashpoint for the most significant division in Mennonite history. (1 Cor. 5:11; 2 Thess. 3:14)

Article 15 — The Oath. Following the Schleitheim tradition and the plain command of the Sermon on the Mount, the Dordrecht Confession forbids the swearing of oaths in any form. The Christian's word must always be reliable; a simple "yes" or "no" must suffice. This prohibition had significant practical consequences in the civic and legal life of early modern Europe and America, where oaths of loyalty, court testimony, and public office were all conducted under oath.

Article 16 — Nonresistance. The Confession articulates the Anabaptist commitment to nonresistance with particular care: "as regards revenge and resistance to enemies with the sword, we believe and confess, that the Lord Christ has forbidden his disciples all revenge and resistance, and has thereby commanded them not to 'return evil for evil, nor railing for railing'; but to 'put up the sword into the sheath,' or, as the prophets have predicted, 'beat them into ploughshares.'" Christians may not take up the sword to defend themselves or their community, may not serve as soldiers, and may not participate in capital punishment. The way of Christ is the way of the cross — suffering, not retaliation. (Matt. 5:39; Rom. 12:17–21)

sections.heading: Legacy and the Amish Division
sections.html:
Legacy and the Amish Division

The Dordrecht Confession's most dramatic historical consequence was a division it did not cause but helped crystallize: the split of the Amish from the Swiss Mennonites in 1693. The Amish schism was precipitated by the ministry of Jacob Ammann (c. 1644–c. 1730), a Swiss Mennonite minister who became convinced that the Swiss congregations were not practicing the Dordrecht Confession's strictures on avoidance with sufficient rigor.

Ammann's position was straightforward: the Dordrecht Confession specified that excommunicated members were to be avoided "at the table and in the marriage bed," and this was not a metaphor or a general principle — it was a literal requirement. Swiss Mennonite leaders, particularly in the Emmental region, argued that avoidance of this kind was too severe, that it destroyed families, and that the biblical texts about avoidance did not require exclusion from one's own household table. Ammann held firm: the Confession was clear, the teaching of Christ and Paul was clear, and laxity in discipline was the root cause of the Swiss congregations' spiritual decline.

The resulting rupture, which took place through a series of conferences and mutual excommunications in 1693, divided the Swiss-South German Mennonite communities between those who followed Ammann (the Amish, Amisch Mennoniten) and those who maintained the older, less strict discipline. Both groups continued to accept the Dordrecht Confession as their authoritative doctrinal statement — the division was over its application, not its authority. This makes the Amish schism uniquely instructive: a community divided over how strictly to apply a document that both sides formally accepted.

The Dordrecht Confession crossed the Atlantic with the first Mennonite emigrants to North America. The settlement of Germantown, Pennsylvania (1683) — largely by Mennonites and Quakers from the Rhineland — was the first Mennonite presence in America. The early eighteenth century brought waves of both Mennonites and Amish from the Palatinate, Switzerland, and the Alsace to Pennsylvania, where William Penn's promise of religious liberty made settlement both possible and attractive. Amish bishops formally signed the Dordrecht Confession at a conference in 1725, cementing its status as the authoritative confession of the American Amish communities.

In modern Anabaptist-Mennonite scholarship, the Dordrecht Confession occupies an ambiguous position. Old Order Amish and Old Order Mennonites continue to regard it as their authoritative confessional statement. Mainstream Mennonite conferences have moved toward more explicitly theological documents (the Mennonite Confession of Faith of 1963 and the Mennonite Church USA's Confession of Faith in a Mennonite Perspective of 1995), while honoring Dordrecht as a historical foundation. The confession's legacy includes both its specific doctrines — believers' baptism, the Lord's Supper as memorial, footwashing, avoidance of worldliness, nonresistance, and separation from the world — and its broader witness to the free-church vision of what the community of Christ should look like.

The theological contributions of the Anabaptist-Mennonite tradition to the wider church have been disproportionate to its size. Its insistence on believers' baptism planted the seed of what would become the Baptist movement in England and ultimately the largest Protestant denomination in the world. Its theology of nonresistance and peacemaking has become an increasingly important resource for Christian ethics in the age of modern warfare. Its practice of community economics — mutual aid, simplicity of life, the subordination of individual wealth to communal need — stands as a prophetic challenge to consumerist Christianity. And its fundamental instinct — that the church is a gathered community of disciples, voluntarily covenanting together under the lordship of Christ, distinct from the world it serves — remains a vital corrective to every form of Constantinian Christianity that identifies the kingdom of God with any earthly power or institution. (Rom. 12:18; Matt. 5:9)
